items.requirement_raw: All security policies and operational procedures identified in Requirement 3 are documented, kept up to date, in use, and known to all affected parties.
items.question: Do you have written procedures for protecting stored card data, and do staff know and follow them?
items.help_text: This applies if you keep any paper records with account data, like receipts or printed reports. Your policy can be simple, but it should be documented, current, used in daily work, and communicated to the people who handle those records.

items.requirement_raw: Sensitive authentication data (SAD) is not stored after authorization, even if encrypted. All sensitive authentication data received is rendered unrecoverable upon completion of the authorization process.
items.question: After a transaction is approved, do you make sure sensitive card-authentication data is not kept anywhere?
items.help_text: Sensitive authentication data includes things like track data, card verification code, and PIN-related data. Even encrypted copies cannot be kept after authorization is complete.
expected_testing_raw: Observe the secure data deletion processes.

items.requirement_raw: The full contents of any track are not stored upon completion of the authorization process.
items.question: Do you make sure full magnetic-stripe or chip track data is never kept after the transaction is authorized?
items.help_text: You may need to keep limited card details like cardholder name, PAN, expiration date, and service code, but not the full track contents.
evidence_examples: Processor or terminal documentation showing no track storage

items.requirement_raw: The card verification code is not stored upon completion of the authorization process.
items.question: Do you make sure the 3- or 4-digit card security code is never kept after the transaction is finished?
items.help_text: If staff write down the card security code during a transaction, it must be securely destroyed right after the sale or fully blacked out before the paper is stored. If you never collect this code, this may be Not Applicable.
evidence_examples: Procedure for destroying or blacking out CVV notes

items.requirement_raw: The personal identification number (PIN) and the PIN block are not stored upon completion of the authorization process.
items.question: Do you make sure PINs and PIN blocks are never stored after the transaction is authorized?
items.help_text: PIN blocks may be encrypted during normal processing, but they still cannot be stored after authorization is complete.
evidence_examples: Processor or terminal documentation showing no PIN storage

items.requirement_raw: PAN is masked when displayed so only personnel with a legitimate business need can see more than the BIN and last four digits.
items.question: When card numbers are shown on screens, printouts, or receipts, do you hide most digits so only authorized staff can see the full number?
items.help_text: Normally only the BIN and last 4 digits should be visible. This applies to displays of the card number, not to how stored card data is protected elsewhere.
expected_testing_raw: Examine the documented list of roles that need access to more than the BIN and last four digits of the PAN.
Examine displays of PAN (for example, on screen, on paper receipts).
evidence_examples: Sample receipt or screen showing masked PAN
List of roles allowed to see full PAN

sections.section_title: Restrict Access by Business Need to Know

items.requirement_raw: Access is assigned to users, including privileged users, based on job classification and function, and least privileges necessary to perform job responsibilities.
items.question: Do you give employees only the minimum access they need to do their jobs?
items.help_text: Access should be based on role and job duties. Even privileged users should not have more access than necessary.
expected_testing_raw: Examine user access settings, including for privileged users.
Interview personnel responsible for assigning access.

sections.section_title: Restrict Physical Access to Cardholder Data

items.requirement_raw: All media with cardholder data is physically secured.
items.question: If you keep paper records with card data, are they stored in a physically secure place?
items.help_text: Examples include a locked drawer, locked cabinet, or safe. For SAQ B, this section applies to paper records containing account data.
evidence_examples: Photo or description of locked storage

items.requirement_raw: Offline media backups with cardholder data are stored in a secure location.
items.question: If you keep offline backup media with card data, is it stored in a secure location?
items.help_text: This is about backup media stored off-network or off-system. The storage location itself should be secure and controlled.
expected_testing_raw: Interview responsible personnel at the storage location(s).
evidence_examples: Photo or description of locked storage

items.requirement_raw: All media with cardholder data is classified in accordance with the sensitivity of the data.
items.question: Do you label or otherwise classify paper or other media with card data according to how sensitive it is?
items.help_text: The goal is to make sure staff understand that media containing card data must be handled more carefully than ordinary business documents.
expected_testing_raw: Examine media logs or other documentation.
evidence_examples: Photo or description of locked storage

items.requirement_raw: Media with cardholder data sent outside the facility is sent by secured courier or other delivery method that can be accurately tracked.
items.question: If paper or other media with card data leaves your location, do you send it using a secure and trackable delivery method?
items.help_text: You should be able to track where the media went and confirm it was sent securely.
expected_testing_raw: Examine offsite tracking logs for all media.
evidence_examples: Photo or description of locked storage

items.requirement_raw: Management approves all media with cardholder data that is moved outside the facility (including when media is distributed to individuals).
items.question: Does management approve any movement of paper or other media with card data outside your facility?
items.help_text: Approval should come from someone with enough authority to approve the transfer, even if 'manager' is not their job title.
evidence_examples: Photo or description of locked storage

items.requirement_raw: Hard-copy materials with cardholder data are destroyed when no longer needed, using cross-cut shredding, incineration, or pulping, and are stored in secure containers before destruction.
items.question: When paper records with card data are no longer needed, do you destroy them securely and keep them protected until destruction?
items.help_text: Acceptable destruction methods include cross-cut shredding, pulping, or incineration. Until destruction, the paper should stay in a secure storage container.
evidence_examples: Photo or description of locked storage

items.requirement_raw: POI devices are protected from tampering and unauthorized substitution, including maintaining a list of devices, periodic inspections, and training personnel.
items.question: Do you protect payment terminals from tampering or unauthorized replacement?
items.help_text: For SAQ B, this includes keeping a device list, inspecting devices regularly, and training staff to notice suspicious behavior or device changes.

items.requirement_raw: An up-to-date list of POI devices is maintained, including make/model, location, and serial number or other unique identification.
items.question: Do you keep an up-to-date inventory of all payment terminals, including make, model, location, and serial number or another unique ID?
items.help_text: This device inventory helps you spot missing, moved, or substituted terminals.
expected_testing_raw: Examine the list of POI devices.
Observe POI devices and device locations.

items.requirement_raw: POI device surfaces are periodically inspected to detect tampering and unauthorized substitution.
items.question: Do you regularly inspect payment terminals for signs of tampering or unauthorized replacement?
items.help_text: The inspection should be periodic and documented enough that staff know what to look for and how often to check.

items.requirement_raw: Training is provided for personnel in POI environments to be aware of attempted tampering or replacement of POI devices.
items.question: Are employees trained to spot suspicious activity around payment terminals and report possible tampering or replacement?
items.help_text: Training should cover verifying repair personnel, making sure devices are not installed or replaced without verification, noticing suspicious behavior, and reporting concerns quickly.
expected_testing_raw: Review training materials for personnel in POI environments.

sections.section_title: Support Information Security with Policies and Programs

items.requirement_raw: An overall information security policy is established, published, maintained, and disseminated to all relevant personnel, vendors, and business partners.
items.question: Do you have an overall security policy, and has it been shared with the people and vendors who need to follow it?
items.help_text: The policy can be simple for a small business, but it should exist, be kept current, and be distributed to the right people.

items.requirement_raw: The information security policy is reviewed at least once every 12 months and updated as needed to reflect changes to business objectives or risks.
items.question: Do you review your security policy at least once a year and update it when your business or risks change?
items.help_text: An annual review is the minimum. Update sooner if payment processes, vendors, terminals, or security risks change.

items.requirement_raw: The security policy clearly defines information security roles and responsibilities for all personnel, and all personnel are aware of and acknowledge their responsibilities.
items.question: Does your security policy clearly explain who is responsible for protecting card data and do staff understand those responsibilities?
items.help_text: Responsibilities can be simple and based on job level, like owner/manager duties versus clerk duties, as long as they are clearly defined and understood.

items.requirement_raw: A formal security awareness program is implemented to make all personnel aware of the information security policy and procedures, and their role in protecting cardholder data.
items.question: Do you have an ongoing security awareness program that reminds staff how to protect card data and payment devices?
items.help_text: For a small merchant, this can be simple, like a back-office poster, a short checklist, or periodic email reminders, as long as staff are regularly reminded of the rules.

items.requirement_raw: A list of all third-party service providers (TPSPs) with which account data is shared or that could affect the security of account data is maintained, including a description of the services provided.
items.question: Do you keep a list of third-party vendors that handle card data or could affect the security of card data, along with what each one does?
items.help_text: Examples might include processors, gateways, document-storage vendors, remote support vendors, or other service providers that affect your card-data environment.

items.requirement_raw: Written agreements with TPSPs are maintained and include acknowledgments that the TPSP is responsible for the security of account data it possesses or could impact.
items.question: Do you have written agreements with relevant vendors that clearly state their responsibility for protecting card data?
items.help_text: This does not have to use exact PCI wording, but the agreement should make the vendor's security responsibility clear in writing.

items.requirement_raw: An established process is implemented for engaging TPSPs, including proper due diligence prior to engagement.
items.question: Before hiring a vendor that affects card-data security, do you perform due diligence using a documented process?
items.help_text: This means you have a repeatable way to review vendors before engaging them, rather than making ad hoc decisions.

items.requirement_raw: A program is implemented to monitor TPSPs’ PCI DSS compliance status at least once every 12 months.
items.question: Do you check at least once a year that relevant vendors are still meeting their PCI responsibilities?
items.help_text: If a vendor is responsible for part of your PCI scope, you need a process to confirm their compliance status regularly.

items.requirement_raw: Information is maintained about which PCI DSS requirements are managed by each TPSP, which are managed by the entity, and any that are shared.
items.question: Do you clearly document which PCI responsibilities belong to your vendors, which belong to you, and which are shared?
items.help_text: This avoids gaps or misunderstandings between your business and third parties.

items.requirement_raw: An incident response plan exists and is ready to be activated in the event of a suspected or confirmed security incident.
items.question: Do you have an incident response plan that staff can use right away if there is a suspected or confirmed security incident?
items.help_text: For a small merchant, this can be a simple emergency document listing who to contact and what to do. It should be easy for staff to find and use when needed.
expected_testing_raw: Examine documentation from previously reported incidents.
